text:
Department of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Amelia Clarke  
St Bartholomew’s Hospital  
Department of Neurology, West Smithfield, London EC1A 7BE  

Dear Dr Amelia Clarke,  

Michael Turner, DOB: 22-03-1990, Hospital No: H492817 NHS No. 9438257612  
Flat 3, 12 Arundel Court, Shoreditch, London E2 7PA 

Diagnoses: 

Focal epilepsy with focal impaired-awareness seizures, onset 2017. MRI brain (2018) normal. Interictal EEG (2019) left temporal sharp waves. 

Current Medication: 

Levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily  
Lacosamide 100 mg twice daily 

Plan: 

- Optimise antiseizure therapy: increase Lacosamide to 150 mg twice daily, continue Levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily  
- Seizure diary and trigger management; occupational risk mitigation advice provided  
- DVLA guidance discussed and workplace safety measures advised given strobe/bright light exposure  
- Occupational Health referral and employer liaison letter offered (with patient consent)  
- Follow-up in 8–10 weeks, sooner if events worsen 

I reviewed Michael Turner, a stage lighting technician with frequent exposure to strobe and bright lighting, in my clinic today. He reports a longstanding history of focal epilepsy, supported by prior EEG evidence of left temporal sharp waves and a structurally normal MRI. Following the recent medication adjustment, focal impaired-awareness seizures have been occurring more often, several times per week, whereas previously they only happened once or twice a month. He describes a typical warning of a rising epigastric sensation and déjà vu, followed by impaired awareness and automatisms lasting approximately 1–2 minutes, with post-event fatigue. Colleagues have occasionally witnessed episodes during rigging and rehearsal, prompting safety concerns in the work environment. There have been no generalised tonic-clonic seizures. 

He denies medication non-adherence, sleep deprivation is intermittent during show runs, and he has noted that high-frequency strobe sequences and prolonged bright light exposure can precede events. There is no new head injury, intercurrent illness, or alcohol excess. Neurological examination today was normal. 

We discussed optimising therapy given the clear increase in event frequency temporally associated with the recent regimen change. I have advised increasing Lacosamide to 150 mg twice daily while continuing Levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily, with monitoring for dizziness, diplopia, mood change, or rash. He will maintain a detailed seizure diary noting timing, potential light exposure patterns, sleep, hydration, and missed meals. 

Given his role and potential exposure to strobe effects, I provided tailored workplace advice: avoid working at height, hot truss, and live power tasks without direct supervision; avoid solo work during programming; and request colleagues to use a calm verbal cue, protect him from falls, and time events if witnessed. We discussed DVLA guidance owing to focal impaired-awareness seizures, and I encouraged him to review the current regulations and contact the DVLA as applicable. With consent, I have offered to write to Occupational Health and his employer to support reasonable adjustments and risk mitigation. 

I will review him in 8–10 weeks to assess response to the dose adjustment. He will contact the clinic sooner if seizures escalate, become prolonged, or if any new red flag features occur. 

With best wishes,

entities.text: Following the recent medication adjustment, focal impaired-awareness seizures have been occurring more often, several times per week, whereas previously they only happened once or twice a month

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit statements of current seizure frequency. The letter states: "Following the recent medication adjustment, focal impaired-awareness seizures have been occurring more often, several times per week, whereas previously they only happened once or twice a month." The current frequency is described as "several times per week" without a precise number, so per rules use 'multiple' with the highest stated frequency. No other conflicting frequencies are given. Therefore, normalize to 'multiple per week'.
reference: Following the recent medication adjustment, focal impaired-awareness seizures have been occurring more often, several times per week, whereas previously they only happened once or twice a month
